points: When precise measurements are needed, we divide units into smaller parts: 1 unit = 10 tenths (1/10), and 1 tenth = 10 hundredths (1/100); this allows measurement of lengths like 2 7/10 cm or 4 3/10 and 5/100 units accurately.
A tenth (0.1) and a hundredth (0.01) follow the same base-10 principle used in whole numbers, making it easy to add and subtract decimals by arranging digits by place value.
Zeros after the decimal point do not change value (0.2 = 0.20 = 0.200), but zeros in the middle do matter (0.2 ≠ 0.02); trailing zeros can be omitted when writing decimal numbers.

summary.category: Decimal Place Value System and Notation
points: The decimal point (.) marks the separation between whole numbers and fractional parts; place values to the right of the decimal point are 10 times smaller than the place value to their left, extending the Indian place-value system to numbers smaller than 1.
A number like 234 tenths equals 23.4 in decimal form, and 234 hundredths equals 2.34; understanding how to express fractional units as decimals is crucial for accurate representation and comparison.

The decimal system is based on 10 because it naturally extends the place-value principle: each place is 10 times the next (ones, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, etc.), making calculations and conversions systematic and manageable.
To compare decimals, compare digits at each place value from left to right; the first difference found (e.g., 6.456 < 6.465 because 5 < 6 in the hundredths place) determines which number is larger.
In the metric system, 1 cm = 10 mm, 1 m = 100 cm, 1 kg = 1000 g; these base-10 relationships make metric conversions straightforward using decimal notation (e.g., 56 mm = 5.6 cm, 254 g = 0.254 kg).

Decimals can be located precisely on a number line by dividing intervals into 10 equal parts; magnifying successive intervals reveals positions of decimals with more decimal places (e.g., locating 4.185).
